channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran Gets Major Economic Lifeline for Minimal Concessions in Initial Deal
item.description: The agreement delays the most difficult steps for Iran for later talks, while granting it crucial benefits.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Planet Labs Pbc, via Reuters
item.media:description: A satellite image of Kharg Island, a crucial Iranian oil hub. The deal grants Iran waivers to begin exporting its oil even before the negotiation of a final agreement on its nuclear program.

item.title: Trump’s Deal With Iran Opens New Rifts in G.O.P.
item.description: Some in the president’s party were skeptical about whether the agreement he reached included adequate concessions from Iranian officials.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner, Robert Jimison and Tim Balk
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump on Wednesday at the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France. Some Republican leaders have balked at the terms of Mr. Trump’s preliminary arrangement to end the war in Iran.

item.title: Before Making a Deal, Trump Demanded Iran’s Surrender. He Got a Surprise.
item.description: While the Iranians suffered substantial losses in the war, they emerged from a confrontation with the world’s most powerful military having proved they can use economic chaos as a weapon.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at a Group of 7 summit in France on Wednesday. The president has argued he has the upper hand with an agreement that leaves negotiations over Iran’s nuclear program for a later date.

item.title: How Iran Could Benefit From Its Deal With the U.S., and an A.I. Arms Race in Schools
item.description: Plus, the “Obamalisk” opens.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Rebecca F. Elliott and Michael Kimmelman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump on Wednesday lashed out at critics who say the Iran agreement achieves less than the one President Barack Obama signed in 2015.

item.title: Ukraine Strikes Moscow Refinery in Large-Scale Drone Attack
item.description: The attack, which shut down the capital’s airports for several hours, appeared to be the biggest wave of strikes on the city since the start of the war.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:credit: via Reuters
item.media:description: The scene over the area of an oil refinery on the outskirts of Moscow on Thursday.

item.title: Critics of Russia Say This Critic Isn’t Critical Enough
item.description: Alexander Sokurov has questioned the Russian president about government repression, but he is still controversial among his country’s exiles.
item.dc:creator: Milana Mazaeva and Neil MacFarquhar
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alexander Sokurov during the screening of his latest film, “Director’s Diary,” in Paris, in March.

item.title: Bill Pulte, a Bulldog for Trump, Prepares to Take Reins at Spy Agency
item.description: Democrats and some Republicans excoriated President Trump’s pick for a top national security job. The president pushed ahead anyway.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Evan Vucci/Associated Press
item.media:description: Bill Pulte has used his current post as a top federal housing official to help with President Trump’s campaign of retribution against his perceived enemies.

item.title: Trump Feuds With Thune and G.O.P., Stoking Election-Year Rift
item.description: The president upended the majority leader’s plans by yanking his intelligence nominee from a confirmation hearing and insisting on an end to the filibuster.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, has shown more willingness in recent days to push back against President Trump no matter the fallout.

item.title: Knicks Accept a White House Invitation to Celebrate Title, Owner Says
item.description: James Dolan said he was “very proud” to bring his championship team to Washington. It would be the first N.B.A. team to visit the White House with Trump as president.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Gregory Shamus/Getty Images
item.media:description: James Dolan, the owner of the Knicks, center, celebrated the team’s championship win on Saturday.

item.title: What to Know if You’re Going to the Knicks Parade
item.description: New Yorkers are descending on Lower Manhattan on Thursday morning to celebrate the Knicks.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan and Sarah Maslin Nir
item.media:credit: Mimi d’Autremont for The New York Times
item.media:description: The parade starts at 10 a.m., but you’ll want to get there at least two hours earlier.

item.title: See the Knicks Championship Parade Unfold
item.description: Fans are gathering in Manhattan on Thursday to cheer on the N.B.A. champions at a parade that has been 53 years in the making.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times and Maya King
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times

item.title: Barack Obama Presidential Center Opens in Chicago
item.description: Today’s opening is expected to draw four former presidents, as well as Bruce Springsteen, Bono and Jennifer Hudson. Chicago has seen years of planning and legal fights.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Obama Presidential Center on Chicago’s South Side.

item.title: Warsh Wants the Fed to Send Fewer Signals. That Comes With Risks.
item.description: Investors piled on bets for higher borrowing costs after Kevin Warsh opted against providing policy guidance at his first meeting as Federal Reserve chairman.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Warsh, the Federal Reserve chair, believes central bank officials should be more guarded in what they share.

item.title: Warsh Makes His Case With Jargon, and a Penchant for Detail
item.description: The Federal Reserve’s new chairman held a press conference where he bantered with reporters and laid out a vision for change at the central bank.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, after the central bank’s meeting in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: When in Jersey for the World Cup, Do as the Locals Do. (Go to a Mall.)
item.description: There is no tailgating at MetLife Stadium, which is surrounded by swampland and highways. But there is a three-million-square-foot megamall nearby.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times

item.title: Why the By-Election in Makerfield Will Have Giant Consequences for the UK
item.description: Here is what to know about the by-election on Thursday in Makerfield, the outcome of which could lead to Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s ouster.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:credit: Temilade Adelaja/Reuters
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, the mayor of Greater Manchester, giving a speech in Wigan, England, on Saturday.

item.title: Inflation Will Linger Despite U.S.-Iran Deal, British Officials Warn
item.description: Bank of England policymakers kept interest rates on hold, saying the impact of the war in Iran was still a major source of uncertainty for the economy.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Policymakers at the Bank of England have held rates steady since late last year, as the war in the Middle East pushed up energy prices.

item.title: Can a Federal Plan Fix Dismal Penn Station? There’s Hope.
item.description: Everyone agrees that Penn Station is a disaster, but not on how to fix it. Now a Trump-backed, multibillion-dollar plan to rebuild it offers a promising step forward.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmelman and Tom Wilson

item.title: The Thriving Black Market for a Powerful Weight Loss Drug
item.description: People are buying what they believe to be retatrutide from WhatsApp groups, Chinese labs and websites selling it “for research purposes.”
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum

item.title: ‘Toy Story 5’ Review: The Machines Have Come for the Children
item.description: In this latest installment of the popular franchise, Woody and Buzz Lightyear are back and must contend with Bonnie’s mesmerizing new toy.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Tornadoes Reported in Wisconsin and Illinois as Violent Storms Sweep the Midwest
item.description: There were early reports of damage in Illinois. The threat of severe weather was expected to continue into Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Retail Investors Help Send SpaceX Shares Soaring
item.description: The company’s blockbuster trading debut is the latest example of how individual investors have become a force in financial markets.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni and Joe Rennison
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Since its stock market debut last Friday, SpaceX has been the most bought stock by retail investors each day.

item.title: Student Cheating Is Becoming Impossible to Detect in an A.I. Era
item.description: Big tech companies and small start-ups are using social media to hype new tools that allow students to trick teachers and A.I. detectors.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein

item.title: What to Know Before Buying SpaceX Stock, According to Financial Advisers
item.description: SpaceX has rapidly become one of the world’s most valuable companies. Even so, experts say investing in single stocks can be risky.
item.dc:creator: Aruni Soni
item.media:credit: Connor Robinson

item.title: New Super PAC, the Guardrails Alliance, Aims to Rally Tech Workers to Help Limit A.I.
item.description: The Guardrails Alliance, which has raised $5 million, is positioning itself as a populist effort that will take on the pro-A.I. interests trying to influence this year’s elections.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shaunna Thomas is a founder of the Guardrails Alliance, a new super PAC trying to take on moneyed entities that oppose reining in artificial intelligence.

item.title: She Saw Her Skydiving Plane Burn. Would She Jump Again?
item.description: Lacy Reynolds was waiting to catch the next flight when the aircraft crashed in Butler, Mo., killing 11 passengers and a pilot. She wasn’t sure she could keep skydiving.
item.dc:creator: Maia Spoto and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Mid America News Review, via Associated Press

item.title: Why Extreme Sports Enthusiasts Keep Jumping After a String of Tragedies
item.description: Often considered thrill seekers looking for the next adrenaline hit, participants say they’re actually drawn to control and a sense of time slowing down.
item.dc:creator: Kurt Streeter and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Austin Johnson/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The aftermath of a crash involving a skydiving aircraft in Butler, Mo., on Sunday.

item.title: JD Vance on the Morality of the Trump Administration
item.description: I asked the vice president what is Christian about this White House.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Victoria Chamberlin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: We Ran the Numbers. Remote Work Is Bad for Us.
item.description: Despite its advantages, work-from-home arrangements have significantly deepened Americans’ isolation and distress.
item.dc:creator: Emma Harrington and Natalia Emanuel
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times

item.title: At the Supreme Court
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Feels ‘Hormuzled’ by Trump’s New Deal With Iran
item.description: Although the agreement finally reopens the Strait of Hormuz, “we threw in a minimum of $300 billion,” Kimmel said. “Right now, Melania’s wondering, ‘How do I get a deal like that?’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: In Southeast Asia, Switching to Solar Offers Relief from Energy Crisis Caused by Iran War
item.description: Homeowners and businesses in the region, which relies heavily on imported oil, are installing rooftop solar panels to reduce the sting of soaring energy costs.
item.dc:creator: Zunaira Saieed and Aie Balagtas See
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Setting up a solar power system at a home in Quezon City, Philippines, this month.
